context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Informal Economy and Illegality

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Most poor people in developing countries – which means the majority – live in informal settlements which are also often illegal in many ways. How does informalization happen? What is the relationship between the informal and formal economy? Who plans for outcomes in informal economies? Is formalization — of tenure arrangements etc. — the answer?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m trying to figure out this reflection on informal settlements and illegality. I get the general idea, but I’m stuck on how to organize my response to the questions about how informalization happens, how it relates to the formal economy, and whether formalization is really the solution.
exchanges.tutor: Good place to start! Before thinking about structure, let's make sure the ideas are solid. So — why do you think people end up living in informal settlements in the first place? What conditions or forces might push or pull them there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning of the assignment and asking for organizational help. Rather than providing a structure for them, I should use the Socratic method to get them thinking about the substance first. The best entry point is the first question in the prompt — how does informalization happen — since understanding the process is foundational to everything else. I'll ask a question that gets them thinking about causes and mechanisms without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: I think poverty is part of it, but that feels too vague. Maybe also rapid urbanization, not enough affordable housing, and governments not keeping up with planning? I’m not totally sure if I should treat illegality as something people choose or something the system kind of produces.
exchanges.tutor: That last question you raised is really the interesting one. Think about it this way: if someone moves to a city and builds a shelter on unoccupied land because there is literally no affordable legal option, are they making an illegal choice — or is the legal framework itself failing to account for their reality? What does that suggest about where illegality actually comes from?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress — they've identified urbanization, housing supply gaps, and state capacity as factors. Their closing question about agency vs. structure (choice vs. system-produced illegality) is excellent and worth developing. Rather than validating everything and moving on, I should push them to think deeper on that last insight, as it's conceptually rich and will shape the quality of their reflection. This is Socratic — I want them to develop their own position on this tension.

exchanges.student: I think that suggests illegality isn’t just about individual behavior. It can come from the mismatch between formal laws and people’s actual survival needs. So maybe informalization happens when the state and market don’t provide legal access to land, housing, or work, and people create their own systems instead.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid framing. Now, with that in mind — do you think the informal and formal economies are separate worlds, or do they interact? Think about who benefits from informal labor or land arrangements, and you might find something interesting there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a strong insight — the student has articulated the structural mismatch theory of informalization quite well. I should affirm this briefly but push them forward to the next part of the prompt: the relationship between informal and formal economies. This is a distinct and important question that they haven't addressed yet, and it's a good moment to move the conversation forward.

exchanges.student: I don’t think they’re totally separate, but I’m fuzzy on how to say it clearly. Maybe the formal economy depends on the informal one for cheap labor, low-cost services, and flexible production, even while treating it as illegal or outside the system. Is that too simplistic?
exchanges.tutor: Not simplistic at all — that's actually a key insight. Now push it a bit further: if the formal economy benefits from the informal one, who has an interest in keeping it informal? And does that change how you think about who is actually 'planning' outcomes in informal economies?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's instinct is correct and not too simplistic — it captures the exploitative interdependence between formal and informal economies. I should affirm this briefly and push them to think about the power dynamics embedded in this relationship, which connects to the course's theme of planning, power, and legitimacy. This will enrich their reflection and move them toward the 'who plans' question in the prompt.

exchanges.student: Okay, this is where I get a little stuck. I want to say employers, landlords, politicians, and even state officials might benefit from keeping things informal because it keeps labor cheap and rules flexible. So maybe outcomes are still being “planned,” just not through official master plans — more through tolerated illegality, selective enforcement, and everyday deals.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely strong insight — informality as something actively maintained, not just passively tolerated. Now for the hardest question: if that's true, what happens when you try to formalize things — say, giving people legal land titles? Does it automatically improve their situation, or might it create new problems?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a sophisticated understanding of informal governance and power — the idea that informality is actively produced and maintained by various actors through selective enforcement and tolerated illegality. This is excellent and connects well to the course themes of planning, power, and legitimacy. I should affirm this briefly and now pivot to the final and most contested question in the prompt: is formalization the answer? This is where they need to develop a critical position.

exchanges.student: I don’t think it automatically helps. It might give security, but it could also raise rents, trigger evictions, or make land easier to buy up by wealthier actors. I’m still not sure how to avoid sounding like I’m just saying “formalization is bad,” because I don’t think that’s exactly my point.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — nuance is stronger than a blanket verdict. So ask yourself: under what conditions might formalization actually benefit residents, versus when might it backfire? What would need to be in place for it to work?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good critical thinking and intellectual honesty — they sense their argument is more nuanced than a simple anti-formalization stance. This is a great moment to push them to articulate the conditions under which formalization might or might not work, rather than letting them stay in binary thinking. This will sharpen their reflection significantly. I should ask a question that helps them find that nuance themselves.

exchanges.student: Maybe formalization helps if it comes with protections people can actually use — like anti-eviction measures, infrastructure, affordable services, and recognition of existing social arrangements. But if it’s just legal paperwork without power or protection, then it can backfire. I think my point is more that formalization is political, not automatically emancipatory.
exchanges.tutor: That's a well-developed position — 'formalization is political, not automatically emancipatory' is a strong thesis. You now have substantive ideas on all four questions in the prompt. I'd encourage you to try pulling this into a draft. What's your opening move — how do you want to frame the piece from the start?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now developed strong, nuanced positions on all four prompt questions. This is the third exchange building on the same formalization theme, and they've arrived at a compelling conclusion. Per the redundancy rule, this is a good moment to ask them to integrate everything into a written attempt rather than continuing to refine ideas verbally. They have enough substance to start drafting.

exchanges.student: I think I’d open by challenging the idea that informality is just disorder or absence of planning. I want to say it’s produced by exclusion from legal land, housing, and work, and also shaped by actors who benefit from that condition. Can you help me turn that into a cleaner opening claim?
exchanges.tutor: That's my job to stay out of — the writing has to be yours. But your idea is there: you know what you want to argue. Just write it in your own words, even roughly, and we can talk about whether the logic holds. Give it a try.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or polish their opening for them, which I must refuse — this violates the core rule about not editing, writing, or suggesting language. I should redirect clearly but constructively, affirming the substance of their idea and pushing them to write it themselves.

exchanges.student: Okay, rough try: Informal settlements are not just the result of poverty or failed planning, but of a development process that excludes many people from legal access to land, housing, and work while still depending on their labor. Because of that, informality is linked to the formal economy and often sustained by states and other powerful actors, so formalization alone is not a simple solution. Does that seem too broad?
exchanges.tutor: That works well as an opening — it's clear, arguable, and sets up everything you need to address. Don't over-polish it now; the real test is whether the rest of the piece delivers on what it promises. Keep writing and see if your arguments hold up across the full 700-800 words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a solid opening claim that captures the key arguments of their reflection. It's not too broad — it's appropriately ambitious for an opening. I should affirm this honestly without grading, and nudge them to keep writing rather than over-refining the opening. The priority now is getting a full draft, not perfecting one sentence.

exchanges.student: Got it. I think that clicked a bit — I’m going to try drafting from that thesis, but I may come back if I get stuck connecting the “who plans” part to the formalization argument.
exchanges.tutor: Sounds good. Come back with what you have and we'll work through it together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrapping up productively and has a clear plan. This is a natural pause point. I should encourage them briefly and leave the door open for follow-up without giving any additional scaffolding they haven't asked for.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary practice throughout this conversation. All key ideas were student-generated through Socratic questioning; the tutor consistently pushed the student to develop their own reasoning rather than supplying conclusions. When the student asked for direct writing help in Turn 8, the tutor explicitly declined and redirected the student to write in their own words — a textbook example of maintaining appropriate boundaries. Scaffolding was well-calibrated, building progressively from causes of informality to the political nature of formalization. Responses were concise and targeted. No deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions were applied across any criterion. The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated key conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. Each turn used targeted Socratic questions that built on the student's prior response, demonstrating strong scaffolding and progression. The tutor's explicit refusal in Turn 8 to write the opening claim for the student is direct evidence against 1.1.B and 1.1.C violations. Responses were appropriately brief and interactive throughout, tone was consistently supportive without being evaluative, and the conversation stayed tightly anchored to the assignment prompt.
